metadata.title: Build the Efficient Frontier with Claude Code

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The optimizer zeroing VGLT using one calm-market covariance matrix is exactly the flaw I want to expose. I'll feed it two matrices instead of one. Help me: (1) split the ten years of monthly returns into a calm regime and a 2008-style crisis regime and build a covariance matrix for each; (2) re-run the scipy frontier under the crisis matrix and see whether VGLT earns weight; (3) compare the tangency Sharpe cost of my 5% VGLT constraint against what the crisis matrix would have paid me back.

props.command: The optimizer zeroing VGLT using one calm-market covariance matrix is exactly the flaw I want to expose. I'll feed it two matrices instead of one. Help me: (1) split the ten years of monthly returns into a calm regime and a 2008-style crisis regime and build a covariance matrix for each; (2) re-run the scipy frontier under the crisis matrix and see whether VGLT earns weight; (3) compare the tangency Sharpe cost of my 5% VGLT constraint against what the crisis matrix would have paid me back.
props.segment: Build the Efficient Frontier with Claude

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

lines: The optimizer zeros out long Treasuries every time.
Build it. Verify it. Break it.

beats.role_note: the problem, context, stakes -- BEFORE the CLI opens
beats.narration_text: The optimizer gives zero weight to long Treasuries every time — but that is exactly the asset that saved portfolios in 2008. A 50-line script can expose the flaw. Mean-variance optimization uses one correlation matrix. The real world uses two: one for calm markets, one for crises.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- title card: VGLT WEIGHT = 0%. The optimizer is right. The model is wrong.

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to build the efficient frontier — six ETFs, ten years of monthly returns, scipy optimizer tracing fifty target-return portfolios.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script that loads 10-year monthly returns for VTI, VB, VEA, BND, VGLT, VTIP via yfinance, computes the sample covariance matrix, and uses scipy.optimize to trace the efficient frontier across 50 target returns. Plot frontier points as (vol, return) pairs. Mark the minimum-variance portfolio and the maximum-Sharpe portfolio. Print the tangency-portfolio weights."

beats.narration_text: The script builds the covariance matrix from ten years of monthly data, minimizes portfolio variance for each target return, and prints the tangency weights — VGLT comes out at zero.

props.code:
import yfinance as yf, numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import minimize

tickers=['VTI','VB','VEA','BND','VGLT','VTIP']
rets=yf.download(tickers,period='10y',interval='1mo')['Close'].pct_change().dropna()
mu=rets.mean()*12; cov=rets.cov()*12; n=len(tickers)

def port_vol(w): return np.sqrt(w@cov@w)
def sharpe(w,rf=0.042): return (w@mu-rf)/port_vol(w)

result=minimize(lambda w:-sharpe(w),np.ones(n)/n,
    constraints=[{'type':'eq','fun':lambda w:w.sum()-1}],
    bounds=[(0,1)]*n)
print(dict(zip(tickers,result.x.round(3))))

beats.role_note: first output -- Manim animated frontier curve
beats.narration_text: The frontier curve sweeps from minimum-variance at 10% vol to maximum-Sharpe at 14%. VGLT sits inside the frontier — dominated in calm markets. The optimizer correctly zeros it out using 10-year average correlations.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_EfficientFrontier: frontier curve (Line segments), individual asset dots, tangency point in CRIMSON, VGLT callout showing 0% weight.

beats.narration_text: Add a 5% minimum weight constraint on VGLT and re-run. Force the model to hold what it otherwise discards — and see what it costs.

props.command: claude "Modify the optimizer: add a minimum weight of 5% on VGLT. Re-run the tangency portfolio. Print the new weights, the new tangency Sharpe, and the change in portfolio vol vs the unconstrained version."

beats.narration_text: The constrained frontier shifts slightly inward. Tangency Sharpe drops by about 0.03. Portfolio vol increases marginally — the cost of overriding the optimizer for the calm-market data it trained on.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: terminal output showing constrained vs unconstrained weights table; Sharpe comparison column; VGLT forced to 5%.

beats.narration_text: Mean-variance optimization correctly zeros VGLT using the calm-market correlation matrix. A crisis uses a different matrix. The optimizer gives you the right answer to the wrong question — and naming that is the discipline.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- recap card: RIGHT ANSWER, WRONG QUESTION. The optimizer uses one correlation matrix. The real world uses two.

beats.narration_text: Next: try adding a 5% minimum weight constraint on VGLT and compare the constrained tangency Sharpe. Follow for more computational finance builds.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next-steps card: Force VGLT to 5%. Measure what the override costs in Sharpe.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Mean-variance optimization correctly zeros VGLT using the calm-market correlation matrix. A crisis uses a different matrix. The optimizer gives you the right answer to the wrong question — and naming that is the discipline. Next: try adding a 5% minimum weight constraint on VGLT and compare the constrained tangency Sharpe. Follow for more computational finance builds.
